examples.title: Overfitting vs. Good Fit Demo
examples.description: Demonstrate overfitting with a high-degree polynomial vs. a simple linear fit.
examples.code:
import numpy as np

# Generate noisy data
np.random.seed(42)
X = np.linspace(0, 1, 10)
y_true = 2 * X + 1
y_noisy = y_true + np.random.normal(0, 0.3, len(X))

# Good fit: degree 1 (linear)
coeffs_1 = np.polyfit(X, y_noisy, 1)
pred_1 = np.polyval(coeffs_1, X)
train_err_1 = np.mean((y_noisy - pred_1) ** 2)

# Overfit: degree 9 (fits every point)
coeffs_9 = np.polyfit(X, y_noisy, 9)
pred_9 = np.polyval(coeffs_9, X)
train_err_9 = np.mean((y_noisy - pred_9) ** 2)

# Test on new point
X_test = np.array([0.5])
y_test = 2 * 0.5 + 1 # true value = 2.0

print(f"Linear - Train MSE: {train_err_1:.4f}, Test pred: {np.polyval(coeffs_1, X_test)[0]:.2f}")
print(f"Degree9 - Train MSE: {train_err_9:.4f}, Test pred: {np.polyval(coeffs_9, X_test)[0]:.2f}")
print(f"True value at x=0.5: {y_test:.2f}")
examples.explanation: The degree-9 polynomial has near-zero training error but may give wild predictions on new data (overfitting). The linear model generalizes better despite slightly higher training error.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n * d^2) for polynomial fitting of degree d

examples.title: Simple K-Fold Cross-Validation
examples.description: Demonstrate k-fold splitting to detect overfitting.
examples.explanation: K-fold cross-validation splits data into k folds, using each as a test set once. This helps detect overfitting by evaluating on unseen data.
